title: Laying mosaic tile sheet on a bathroom wall
subtitle: Bed and grout mesh-backed mosaic sheets on a tiled or primed surface
excerpt: How to apply mesh-backed ceramic or glass mosaic tile sheets to a bathroom wall, including setting out the layout, applying the correct adhesive, aligning sheets so grout joints are consistent, cutting edge pieces, and grouting the finished surface.
sourceNotes: British Ceramic Tile Council tiling guidance. Collins Complete DIY Manual. BAL Adhesives technical data.

glossaryTerms.definition: A factory panel of small mosaic tiles (typically 25 × 25 mm or 48 × 48 mm) bonded face-up to a fibreglass mesh backing. The sheet is handled and bedded as a single unit; the adhesive keys through the mesh to the substrate behind.

A fine-grained grout without sand aggregate, used for grout joints narrower than 3 mm. Mosaic tile sheets typically have 1.5 to 2 mm joints between tiles, which require unsanded grout; a sanded mix tears out under the trowel and leaves a rough joint.

An adhesive application method where blobs of tile adhesive are placed at the four corners and centre of each mesh-backed sheet rather than combing across the whole surface. Used for sheets over 300 × 300 mm to allow any air pockets to be pressed out as the sheet is bedded.

content.text: Mosaic tile sheets are handled as a single panel, but each joint between individual tiles still needs to align with the joint on the sheet next to it. The challenge is that slight rotational drift across several sheets accumulates into a visible misalignment by the end of a run. Working with a batten and checking level frequently is essential.
are fixed with a flexible tile adhesive; the adhesive must squeeze up through the mesh to embed each tile fully without bridging the gap between tiles.

The wall must be flat, firm, and free of dust or grease. On bare plasterboard, prime with tile primer before tiling: raw plasterboard draws moisture out of the adhesive before it can key properly. On an existing tiled surface, check that all existing tiles are solid (tap each one; a hollow sound means movement) and sand any glossy grout lines with the sanding block to give the adhesive a key. Remove any loose grout with the scraper.

Find the centre of the wall and mark a vertical plumb line with the spirit level and pencil. Work out from this line in both directions, allowing for the narrow joint between each sheet. Dry-lay a row of sheets along the floor or worktop to check how the layout meets the edges: adjust the starting point so that cut strips at each side are equal and not less than half a sheet wide. Fix a temporary batten level across the bottom of the tiled area to support the first row and keep it from sliding before the adhesive grabs.
Step 3, apply the adhesive and bed the sheets
Apply a flexible tile adhesive to the wall with a notched trowel (3 mm notch for mosaic sheets), working a section at a time. Press each

and press firmly across the whole face with the flat of the hand or a rubber-faced float. Check the sheet is level, then check that the grout joints align with the sheet beside it. A 1.5 to 2 mm plastic spacer tile pushed into the joint between two sheets keeps the inter-sheet gap consistent.

Mark the cut line on the face of the sheet with the combination square and pencil. Cut the mesh with the utility knife, separating individual tiles if needed. Individual mosaic tiles can be nibbled to shape with tile nippers for curved cuts around pipework. On glass mosaic, score individual tiles with a tungsten-carbide tile scorer and snap cleanly over a tile spacer edge. Wear eye protection when snapping glass tiles: they shatter rather than snapping cleanly.
Allow the adhesive to cure for at least 24 hours. Mix

to a smooth paste and apply across the tiled surface with the scraper, working it into all the joints between tiles. Leave for 5 to 10 minutes until the grout begins to go off at the surface, then wipe off the excess with a damp sponge, rinsing frequently. Do not wash the grout out of the joints: wipe lightly with a near-dry sponge. Allow to cure fully before wiping off the haze with a dry cloth.

In a shower enclosure or anywhere subjected to continuous water, use a flexible waterproof tile adhesive and epoxy grout. Standard cement grout is porous and will absorb moisture in a wet shower; epoxy grout is fully waterproof and stain-resistant. For stone mosaic sheets on a floor, seal the stone before grouting to prevent grout staining the porous tile face.

attrs.symptom: Grout joints between adjacent sheets are wider than the joints within sheets
attrs.cause: Sheets not butted up closely enough; inter-sheet spacer not used or removed too late.
attrs.fix: Use consistent 1.5 mm tile spacers between sheets and remove them as soon as the sheet is bedded and before the adhesive goes off.

attrs.symptom: Individual tiles lift off the mesh when the sheet is pressed onto the wall
attrs.cause: Adhesive too stiff or beginning to skin; excess adhesive bridging between tiles and pulling them off the mesh.
attrs.fix: Work with freshly mixed adhesive and apply only what can be covered in 10 to 15 minutes. If tiles are pulling off, press them back individually before the adhesive cures.

attrs.cause: Grout allowed to cure too long before first wipe; surface wiped when too wet so grout was redistributed rather than removed.
attrs.fix: Use a proprietary grout haze remover or a dilute solution of white vinegar on ceramic and porcelain. Do not use acid-based cleaners on natural stone or glass mosaic.
